coredata.dc:title: Non-toxic carbon dots fluorescence sensor based on chitosan for sensitive and selective detection of Cr (VI) in water
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Yaqian
Li, Ruxue
Zhou, Peng
Duan, Chunying
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence carbon dots (CS-CDs) were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal approach using chitosan and urea as precursors. CS-CDs exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity of fluorescence response to Cr (VI) in a wide range with a detection limit of 0.59 μM. More interestingly, CS-CDs-based fluorescent hydrogel sensors were further developed for quantitative detection of Cr (VI) in water. The fluorescence signals of the composite were recorded by a smartphone and converted to grayscale parameters linearly related to concentration of Cr (VI) by Image J software. This composite showed a broad linear range of 10–300 μM and a low detection limit of 2.83 μM. All results demonstrate the great potential for the portable, low-cost and green detection of Cr (VI) in practical application.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Cr (VI)
Fluorescence response of the CS-CDs to Cr(VI)
Possible quenching mechanism of CS-CDs by Cr (VI)
Visual detection of Cr (VI) by CDs-hydrogel

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was financially supported by the National Major Scientific Instruments and Equipment Develop Project of China (2012YQ090197) and the National Science Foundation of China (21777017)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CS-CDs (Inset: HRTEM). (b) The particle size distribution of CS-CDs. (c) Raman spectrum of CS-CDs under 532 nm laser excitation. (d) FTIR spectrum of CS-CDs.
(a) XPS full survey scan and highresolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s and (d) O 1 s for CS-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and the fluorescence spectra of CS-CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CS-CDs upon excitation at different wavelengths. The fluorescence responses of CS-CDs towards different ions in the absence (c) and presence (d) of Cr (VI). (e) The fluorescence spectra of CS-CDs in the presence of different concentration of Cr (VI). Inset: Linear fitting curve of F0/F versus the concentration of Cr (VI) in the range from 1 to 70 μM. (f) Linear fitting curve of log (F0/F) versus the concentration of Cr (VI) in the range from 70 to 400 μM.

(a) Fluorescence decay tests of CS-CDs in the absence and presence of Cr (VI). (b) UV–Vis of Cr (VI) aqueous solution and excitation spectra of CS-CDs. (c) UV–Vis of CS-CDs, Cr (VI), CS-CDs + Cr (VI) system. (d) High-resolution XPS spectra of Cr 2p in CS-CDs + Cr (VI) system.

(a) Photography of CDs-hydrogel composites incubated with different ions. (b) The photography of CDs-hydrogel with the concentration of Cr (VI) increasing from 0 to 500 μM processed by Image J. (c) Linear fitting curve of G0/G versus the concentration of Cr (VI) in the range from 10 to 300 μM.
(a) Schematic diagram of synthesis of CS-CDs and detection of Cr(VI) (b) Schematic illustration of preparation and application of CDs-hydrogel composites.
Comparison with other fluorescence methods for detecting Cr(Ⅵ).
Determination of Cr (VI) in water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Non-toxic carbon dots fluorescence sensor based on chitosan for sensitive and selective detection of Cr (VI) in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalia 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalia 116024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence carbon dots (CS-CDs) were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal approach using chitosan and urea as precursors. CS-CDs exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity of fluorescence response to Cr (VI) in a wide range with a detection limit of 0.59 μM. More interestingly, CS-CDs-based fluorescent hydrogel sensors were further developed for quantitative detection of Cr (VI) in water. The fluorescence signals of the composite were recorded by a smartphone and converted to grayscale parameters linearly related to concentration of Cr (VI) by Image J software. This composite showed a broad linear range of 10–300 μM and a low detection limit of 2.83 μM. All results demonstrate the great potential for the portable, low-cost and green detection of Cr (VI) in practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry, heavy metal pollution has brought serious problems to the natural environment and human health. Chromium is one of the important pollutants in water, mainly from mining, leather kneading, electroplating and other industries [1,2]. The illegal dumping of chromite residues and the discharge of chromium-containing wastewater have brought serious threats to water quality safety. It is well known that the toxicity of chromium is related to its valence. Trivalent chromium is an element beneficial to the human body, while hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)) is toxic. At present, some studies have shown that Cr (VI) has strong teratogenic and carcinogenic properties. When Cr (VI) contacted with the skin, ulcers or allergic reactions will occur. In addition, it can also invade human body through respiratory tract and mucous membrane. Ingesting large doses of Cr (VI) will lead to kidney or liver damage [3–5], bronchiectasis, vomiting and even cancer. In particular, it increases the risk of lung, gastric or colorectal cancer [6–8]. Chromium readily bioaccumulates and is not biodegradable, posing a significant risk to humans and other organisms even at trace concentrations. As a consequence, it is necessary to establish a monitoring system for Cr (VI) in the environment for pollution control and biological protection.

Currently, many analytical techniques are explored to detect Cr (VI), such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), atomic emission spectrometry (AES), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and ion chromatography (IC) [9,10]. However, most of these methods require costly instruments, sophisticated operation and complex sample pretreatments.

Carbon dots (CDs) as a fluorescence sensor can refrain from these issues owing to its low cost, excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis, tunable luminescence and high sensitivity [11–13]. Generally, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon molecules are selected as the best choice to synthesize high-quality CDs [14]. However, aromatic precursors are highly toxic, which limits their application [15]. Therefore, it is still desirable to develop non-toxic precursors to replace toxic organic precursors for the synthesis of CDs. In addition to the advantages of environmental friendliness, biomass also has the advantages of low-cost, rich in heteroatoms, and a wide variety of types [16,17]. Recent years, largely affected by green chemistry concept, biomass was used to produce CDs. Zhang et al. produced green fluorescent CDs deriving from alkali lignin by hydrothermal method and then used to detect Ag+ [18]. Alkali lignin was also synthesized to blue fluorescent CDs through pyrolysis by Shi et al., afterwards applied to the detection of Fe3+ [19]. In addition, Ansi V.A et al. adopted microwave-assisted synthesis to handle table sugar to make cyan emitting CDs, which then served as an environmentally friendly reducing agent for Au (III) [20]. Hence, using green-sourced materials to synthesize CDs is a feasible method.

Furthermore, the current fluorescence detection mainly relies on fluorometers for solution detection. Liquid platform has a short shelf life and is difficult to implement in field environments, limiting its application. On this basis, there is an urgent need to develop a facile and rapid method for sensitive and selective Cr (VI) detection in real samples. The hydrogel has a 3-dimensional matrix and allowed water-soluble components to be transported throughout the matrix. Therefore, the fluorescent CDs can interact with the polymer chains in the hydrogel, and can also interact with the target detection substances. Thus, the composite material of CDs and hydrogel can achieve the fluorescence detection of the target. Inspired by this point, it is planned to develop a portable, quantifiable CDs-hydrogel composite based on CS-CDs.

Chitosan (CS) is a typical natural biomass material, which is widely present in nature, such as the shells of shrimps and crabs that we often discarded [21–23]. There are a number of groups included in CS, such as amino and hydroxyl, which is beneficial to improve CDs performance. It also helps to form interactions with the polymer chains in the hydrogel. In this work, urea and Fe (NO3)3 were chosen as dopant to prepare CS-CDs with chitosan (Scheme 1 a). The CS-CDs, which are rich in amino and hydroxyl on the surface, have been characterized and show good fluorescence properties. It is shown that CS-CDs have excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Cr (VI) detection. After that, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and chitosan were selected to prepared CDs-hydrogel composites together with CS-CDs. And based on this composite, the visualization and quantitative detection of Cr (VI) were successfully realized.

Chitosan (CS) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Urea, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, K2Cr2O7, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, AgNO3, Cd(Ac)2·2H2O, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, Na2S, NaHS, Na5P3O10, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, KClO4, NH4F, NaOH, CH3COOH, HCl, NaCl were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu technology development Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and quinine sulfate were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents and solvents of analytical grades used without further purification. All the experiments were carried out using deionized water.

Analyzed the morphology of CDs through a Transmission Electron Microscopy (JEOL JEM F200). Used Thermo ESCALAB XI + X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy to perform X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. The free radicals of CDs were determined by Bruker vertex-70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. With Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) pattern was characterized with Rigaku D/Max 2400 automatic powder X-ray diffractometer. Multi-wavelength Laser Confocal In-Situ High-resolution Raman Imaging System (LabRAM HR Evolution) was used to get the Raman spectrum. Steady state and time-resolved PL spectra were collected under excitation wavelength of 330 nm and 405 nm respectively on Edinburgh instrument FLS1000 and FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer. TU-1900 spectrophotometer was conducted to perform UV–Vis absorption spectrum.

Generally, the CS-CDs were synthesized by first dissolving 0.136 g chitosan in 10 mL 0.1 M HCl aqueous solution, followed by adding 0.03 g urea and 0.202 g Fe (NO3)3·9H2O. Then, the mixture was taken into a 25 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and then hydrothermally heated at 190 °C for a period of 15 h. Afterwards, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The sample turned into black suspension, after which it is centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the supernatant solution was passed through a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm to remove solid particles. Then the obtained solution was lyophilized to get a brown-black powder.

Simply, for the CDs-hydrogel composite, 2.5 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and 20 mg CS-CDs were dipped in 20 mL DI water by stirring for 0.5 h at room temperature. After that, the mixture was slowly heated to 90 ℃ while stirring until the solution became clear. The chitosan powder (0.1 g) was mixed with PVA solution. After stirring for 0.5 h, the mixture was casted onto a Petri dish or mold and kept at −15 ℃ for 2 h, then unfroze at 25 ℃. Repeated freezing and thawing three times. The CDs-hydrogel composite was prepared (Scheme 1b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the selectivity of CS-CDs, multiple ions (Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr (VI), Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cd2+, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, S2−, HS−, P3O10 5−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, ClO4 − and F− ) were used in the experiments. Ions’ solutions (5 mM) were prepared in DI water firstly. 80 μL solution of each ion and HCl solution (800 μL, 1 M) were mixed with CDs solution (800 μL, 1 mg/mL) and then diluted to 8 mL. Afterwards, we could get a 0.1 mg/mL CS-CDs solution with pH = 1 and 50 μM metal ions. The mixture was allowed to sit at 25 ℃ for 20 min and then transferred to a quartz cuvette, and the fluorescence emission was recorded.

Ion that showed significant fluorescence quenching was further studied to investigate its detection by CDs with the fluorescence titration experiment carrying out. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The measurement of each sample was repeated at least three times. All the experiments were conducted at room temperature.

Several CDs-hydrogels with the same shape and size were placed in different ionic solutions (1 mL) respectively for 30 min to verify the selectivity of CDs-hydrogel. We investigated more than 15 kinds of environmentally relevant ions, as in solution detection. After soaking operation, the photography of CDs-hydrogels put under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp was taken by smart phone.

Then, the effect of Cr (VI) concentration from 0 to 500 μM on the fluorescence of CDs-hydrogels was investigated. The CDs-hydrogels composites were put in solutions of various Cr (VI) concentration (1 mL) respectively for 30 min. Afterwards, Image J was used to process the picture to obtain the relationship between gray value and concentration of Cr (VI). The measurement of each sample was repeated at least three times. All the experiments were conducted at room temperature.

The detection of Cr (VI) in Lingshui river and tap water samples was carried out to investigate the application of CS-CDs in environmental water samples. Before use, water samples from river were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and then passed through 0.22 μm membrane filter. In order to calculate the Cr (VI) recovery based on the detection method of CS-CDs, Cr (VI) standard solutions with different concentrations were added to environmental water samples. According to the constructed standard curve, the concentration of Cr (VI) in water samples was further determined by using the aqueous solution sensing system based on CS CDs.

The quantum yield (QY) of CS-CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as the reference. The equation of QY is:
(1)
Φ u
=
Φ s
×
F u
×
A s
×
η
u
2
F s
×
A u
×
η
s
2
Where
Φ u
and
Φ s
were the fluorescence quantum yields of CS-CDs and standards respectively.
F u
and
F s
stood for the integral of fluorescence intensities of CS-CDs and standards, respectively. The absorbances of the CS-CDs and quinine sulfate at the excitation wavelength were denoted as
A u
and
A s
. The refractive indexes of the solvent dissolving CS-CDs and standards was marked as
η u
and
η s
, respectively.

Firstly, the morphology and size of CS-CDs in aqueous solution were confirmed by the TEM. As displayed in Fig. 1 a, the CS-CDs were spherical in shape. The particle size ranged from 4 to 11 nm, and the average size was calculated as 7.1 nm (Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1a the up inset) showed that the lattice spacing of CS-CDs was 0.23 nm, which implies the (100) crystal facet of graphite [24,25].

Besides, Raman spectrum also demonstrated the crystallinity features. In Raman spectrum (Fig. 1c), the characteristic D bond at 1330 cm−1 represented the defect of carbon atom lattice. And the crystalline G bond at 1575 cm−1 linked the E2g mode of graphite because of the vibration of sp2 hybrid carbon atom. The coexistence of D and G bands proved that CS-CDs were composed of sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons. Therefore, it was confirmed that the surface of CS-CDs is both highly graphitized and amorphous [26,27]. Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. S1, a broad peak centered at around 21.39° in XRD pattern corresponding to the amorphous phases of carbon [28]. At the same time, several obviously sharp peaks at 17.92, 22.36, 28.89, 32.76 and 39.85° was attributed to crystalline phases of carbon, which might accompany by the amorphous phases of carbon formation [29]. According to reports, the abundant oxygen-rich groups in CDs could increase the dispersibility, stability and hydrophilicity of CDs, which explains the great potential of CDs for sensing in aqueous solutions [30].

In order to further explore the functional groups on the CS-CDs surface, FT-IR and XPS were applied. The broad and intense absorption band peaked at 3145 cm−1 could be attributed to the O–H stretching vibration in FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1d). Besides, a characteristic absorption band centered at 1399 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of C-N bond. It was worth noting that the FTIR show three weak absorption bands of stretching vibrations H-C = O at 2788 cm−1, C = O at 1762 cm−1, C = C at 1632 cm−1 [31–33]. In a word, all FTIR signals showed that the obtained CS-CDs is rich in oxygen, carbon and nitrogen. There are many hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CS-CDs, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and so on, which is conducive to enhance their hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous solution.

The XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 ) was also employed to investigate the surface states and elements composition of CS-CDs. The full spectrum (Fig. 2a) presented two dominant peaks at 285.0 eV, 532.3 eV, and two weak peaks at around 400.3 eV, 708.7 eV, corresponding to the carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and iron, respectively. The appearance of Fe2p peak confirmed the successful doping of Fe in CS-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum were displayed in Fig. 2b, 2c, 2d. The C1s spectrum could be distributed to 288.4 eV, 286.3 eV, 285.1 eV and 284.6 eV, which assigned to O-C = O, C-N, C-O and C–C/C = C, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectrum could be split into two peaks located in 401.5 eV and 399.7 eV, corresponding to N–H and C-N-C. The high-resolution O1s spectrum could be fitted into two peaks, proving the existence of the O-C (532.7 eV) and O = C (531.7 eV) [34–37].
To sum up, it can be confirmed that the obtained CS-CDs have not only graphite-like crystal structure but also abundant hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl and amino.

The optical properties of CS-CDs were explored by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrum. The purple line in Fig. 3 a shows that the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CS-CDs in aqueous solution has two distinct peaks around 215 and 290 nm. The intense absorption peak at around 215 nm could be ascribed to the π-π* transition of C = C and another peak could be assigned to the n-π* transition of O/N-contained π-conjugation domain [38,39]. The fluorescence properties also showed in Fig. 3a, while max excitation (330 nm) and emission (408 nm) corresponding to the pink and blue line, respectively. Besides, as described in Fig. 3b, an obvious redshift in λem (395 nm to 465 nm) could be noticed when CDs solution excited from 280 nm to 400 at the gap of 10 nm. This excitation-dependent emission phenomenon might derive from the intrinsic attribute of CS-CDs, such as nonuniform sizes and the existence of various surface energy traps (SETs) [40,41]. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield was calculated with Quinine Sulfate as a reference composite, which is 16.81%.

To further confirm the practicability for sensing applications, we also investigated the photostability of CS-CDs. As displayed in Fig. S2a, when the CS-CDs was exposed to xenon lamp for continuous irradiation, the fluorescence intensity kept almost constant during 120 min, which verifying the extremely good photostability of CS-CDs. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs under different ionic intensities was measured. With the increasing concentration of NaCl, no obvious change was observed as depicted in Fig. S2b. At last, the effects of pH on the fluorescence properties of CS-CDs were examined under different pH conditions. As exhibited in Fig. S2c, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs at 408 nm did not change significantly in the pH range of 2.0–11.0. In summary, these results supported that CS-CDs have excellent optical stability and can be used as high-performance fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the CS-CDs to Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that CDs can be used as a sensitive fluorescent probe to discern various metal ions. Selectivity is an important criterion to judge the detection performance of probes. Therefore, in order to verify the detection performance of CS-CDs, we did a series of experiments under the same conditions to examine the influence of different ions on the fluorescent intensity. More than 15 kinds of ions were investigated, such as Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr (VI), Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cd2+, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, S2−, HS−, P3O10 5−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, ClO4 − and F−. As drawn in Fig. 3c, only Cr (VI) could significantly quench the fluorescence of CS-CDs, rather than other environmentally related ions. In addition, Fig. 3d exhibited that none of the relevant ions can interfere with the detection of Cr (VI) by CS-CDs. Based on these experimental facts, we could confirm that CS-CDs have high selectivity for the fluorescent detection of Cr (VI).

The sensing parameters including concentration of probe and pH were studied to attain the best sensing capability of CS-CDs. Based on the experimental facts, the optimization of the sensing parameters was selected at 0.1 mg/mL of CS-CDs and pH = 1. Then the fluorescent spectrum of titration experiment was implemented to verify the practicability of CS-CDs based sensors for Cr (VI) detection. As depicted in Fig. 3e, the emission intensity at 408 nm when excited at 330 nm was gradually decreased with the addition of Cr (VI) aqueous solution. Calculating the F0/F versus the concentration of Cr (VI) ranging from 1 to 70 μM, a superb linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) was obtained as displayed in the up inset of Fig. 3e with the linear regression equation F0/F = 0.00791C + 1.00941 (C meaning the concentration of Cr (VI)). The limit of detection (LOD) was computed as 0.59 μM according to the formula of 3σ/k(σ meaning the standard deviation of blank sample, k meaning the slope of the linear regression equation). Another linear equation was Log (F0/F) = 0.0025C + 0.03475, where the concentration of Cr (VI) in the range of 70 to 400 μM and R2 = 0.997 (Fig. 3f). The relative sensitivity parameters of CS-CDs to Cr(VI) including LOD and linear range were compared with the published literature listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that CS-CDs has better detection performance for Cr(VI). Moreover, Chinese National Standard requires that the content of Cr (VI) in industrial wastewater should not exceed 0.5 mg/L (9.62 μM). In addition, according to the U.S. EPA guidelines, the minimum carcinogenic concentration of Cr (VI) in drinking water is 0.1 mg/L (1.92 μM). Obviously, both of these criteria are within the detection range of CDs, which means that CS-CDs have great potential for practical application in the detection of Cr(VI) in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of CS-CDs by Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CS-CDs by Cr (VI), the fluorescence decay spectra of CS-CDs in the presence and absence of Cr (VI) were firstly investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the average fluorescence lifetime of CS-CDs and CS-CDs + Cr (VI) were 1.45 ns and 1.34 ns, respectively. The change in fluorescence lifetime upon addition of Cr (VI) is small, indicating that the process is unlikely to be a FRET or dynamic quenching mechanism. Then, the UV–vis spectra of Cr (VI) and the fluorescence spectra of CS-CDs were conducted to further study the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The results in Fig. 4b showed that Cr (VI) has two characteristic absorption peaks at 257 nm and 348 nm, while CS-CDs showed a broad excitation wavelength centered at 330 nm. Obviously, the excitation wavelength of CS-CDs highly overlaps with the absorption spectrum of Cr (VI), which indicates that Cr (VI) can effectively shield the excitation light of CS-CDs. Therefore, we speculated that the inner filter effect (IFE) process should take into consideration.

Moreover, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CS-CDs before and after addition of Cr (VI) were also explored. There was no obvious change in the absorption peaks as shown in Fig. 4c, indicating that no new complexes are formed, and the quenching mechanism of CS-CDs cannot be attributed to the static quenching process. Besides, the XPS of CS-CDs after addition of Cr (VI) was also tested. The appearance of Cr (III) in the fitting results of Fig. 4d indicated that the recognition process may be accompanied by electron transfer. Therefore, based on these above results, the IFE can be the main reason for fluorescence quenching induced by Cr (VI).

To further investigate the practical applications of CS-CDs, the detection of Cr (VI) was implemented in Lingshui river and tap water samples. The accuracy of the method was verified through a recovery experiment by adding a certain amount of Cr (VI) to these samples. By comparing the calculated Cr (VI) concentration (based on the linear regression equation of F0/F versus Cr (VI) concentration) with the spiked concentration at 5, 10 and 20 μM, the recovery of Cr (VI) spiked real samples is obtained, as shown in Table 2 . The recovery of Cr (VI) was between 98.22% and 103.91% for water samples with RSD around 0.93% to 2.72%. This result confirms the feasibility and reliability of CS-CDs in rapid and accurate detection of Cr (VI) in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Cr (VI) by CDs-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Detection in solution requires a complex, sensitive and expensive fluorometer. In order to achieve simpler test, it is vital to develop a portable, quantitative and low-cost sensing method[47,48]. Herein, CDs-hydrogel was contrived as an ideal sensor for Cr (VI) detection. Here, the choice of PVA and chitosan as synthetic gel materials was more stable than that of PVA alone, which was more conducive to subsequent detection. In order to verify its stability, CDs-hydrogel composite was soaked in distilled water and no CS-CDs leakage was found, which proved that the composite was very stable.

After that, we adjusted the content of CDs in the CDs-hydrogel composite to obtain the best conditions of luminescence. As displayed in Fig. S3, from the composite images doped with different concentrations of CDs (0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5 mg/mL), it was obvious that the luminescence of 1 mg/mL and 2 mg/mL composites is the strongest. In order to achieve more sensitive detection, the concentration of 1 mg/mL was determined. Under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, a highly uniform blue luminescence was exhibited by the CDs-hydrogel composite with 1 mg/mL CS-CDs. In addition, the effects of incubation with 100 μM Cr (VI) for 5, 10, 30, 60, 90 and 120 min on the detection were also studied. Meanwhile, Image J was applied to process the photography taken by the smart phone, and the result (gray value versus the incubation time) was shown in Fig. S4. After 30 min of incubation, the fluorescence was significantly quenched, and then the increase of incubation time had little effect on the gray value. Thus, 30 min was determined to be the incubation time for the follow-up experiment.

Selectivity is an important criterion to judge the detection performance of CDs-hydrogel composite. Thus, the CDs-hydrogel composite was further explored to determine the effect of different ions on fluorescence under the same conditions. It was the same as detecting in liquid state that more than 15 kinds of ions were investigated, such as Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr (VI), Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cd2+, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, S2−, HS−, P3O10 5−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, ClO4 − and F−. As demonstrated in the photography got by smart phone (Fig. 5 a), only Cr (VI) could significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs-hydrogel, rather than other ions. Therefore, it is confirmed that CDs-hydrogel could be used to detect Cr (VI) with high selectivity, which meant the prepared CDs-hydrogel composite could be applied in complex environment. Thus, CDs-hydrogel composites have great potential in practical applications.

Then the influence of the concentration of Cr (VI) on fluorescence was investigated, which verified the practicability of CDs-hydrogel based sensors for Cr (VI) detection. As demonstrated in Fig. S5, when the concentration of Cr (VI) increasing from 0 to 500 μM, the blue luminescence of the CDs-hydrogel sensor was quenched. Image J was also applied to process the photography (Fig. 5b). And then it was obtained that an excellent linear relationship between the ratio of gray value (G0/G) and the concentration of Cr (VI) (μM). Fig. 5c exhibited the linearity with the Cr (VI) concentration ranging from 10 to 300 μM (R2 = 0.991) with the linear regression equation G0/G = 0.004C + 0.884 (C meaning the concentration of Cr (VI)), and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 2.83 μM. Thus, based on the facts above, the manufactured CDs-hydrogel composite could be conducted as a quantitative sensor for Cr (VI) detection.

In summary, a novel chitosan-based fluorescent carbon dot was developed by a simple one-step hydrothermal method for efficient selective detection of Cr(VI). The prepared CS-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, high photostability, good hydrophilicity and salt tolerance. The fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs was quantitatively quenched by Cr(VI) in a wide range of 1–400 μM mainly through the IFE mechanism. The limit of detection was 0.59 μM, which is lower than EPA guidelines for drinking water. What's more, the fluorescent hydrogel composites manufactured based on CS-CDs were successfully prepared. The CDs-hydrogel composite can be used as portable sensors for quantitatively detect Cr(VI) in the range of 10–300 μM, with a detection limit as low as 2.83 Μm, which is below the requirements of China’s industrial wastewater discharge standards. This indicates that the CS-CDs hydrogel can also be used for the detection of Cr(VI) concentration in water. Overall, the sensors based on CS-CDs have broad application prospects in water quality monitoring.
Yaqian Feng: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ruxue Li: Investigation. Peng Zhou: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chunying Duan: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the National Major Scientific Instruments and Equipment Develop Project of China (2012YQ090197) and the National Science Foundation of China (21777017)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107627.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
